author: @hissst69

Much like natural gas is multi use today. great video as usual.

author: @colrodrick8784
text:
Always illuminating to watch another of your videos Robert.

While we do still dig up and export a lot of coal (in fact my wonderful step daughter works at a thermal coal mine), I have never seen the coal flakes that you speak of.  We have cans fires each spring nearby which does land on cars and sort of melt.  Perhaps that’s similar.

Interesting subject.

Cheers and thanks

replies.author: @Smo1k
replies.text: Nowadays, coal is mostly used in power plants and they tend to reduce it first, removing as much as possible of anything that isn't carbon, hydrogen or water. What used to seed the sticky, extremely bad tasting flakes was a number of metal (not least the most mercury the human race have ever mishandled) and/or sulphur salts.

author: @unhippy1
text: nearly all of the downsides he speaks of with coal is straight down to badly designed coalburners that have the new batches of fuel heaped on top of the already burning coal......better designs were developed pretty early on that brought the new fuel up from underneath the already burning coal and onto the grate, this type of burner runs with almost no smoke and soot however they were more expensive than a simple box with a fire grate that you put coal in and it burned

author: @TheBaconWizard
text: Great vid, Rob! I strongly feel that one of our great modern mistakes is moving away from a single source of heat in the home. One's hearth would have provided great food, smoked (preserved foods) hot water, and warmth all from one renewable source. As you have proved, it could potentially provide electricity too!

author: @johnmeldrum4717
text:
In answer to your question  Robert ,  micro hydro on floating barges which can rise and fall with floods multiple units on any given stretch of rivers, stream or estuaries..
Far more calcuable than wind . Nondisrubtive to water courses unlike dams . And self cleaning design possible.
Wind is fickle  solar sleeps at night.  Streams and rivers flow constantly and can be measured . Under shot water wheels running in series on floating platforms . Thats a no brainer. No dams  no disruption to the ecology. 
I have designed this and took it to the point of patent . But  I  decided to share it humanity to offer an ecological solution to the energy problem  . Thanks for your efforts and enthusiasm.  Cheers John

author: @totherarf
text:
Traditionally most women would have been working ...... weather at agriculture, or in industry. It may have been cottage industry, say spinning while the man worked the loom or even down the mine. Being a "Housewife" was a relatively new thing. 
I think the level of oppression of both men and women would not easily be believed by modern people.

I remember my mum telling me about putting the washing out in a tiny Salford Yard where they had to keep an eye out for if it rained. Not because of the clothes getting wet but dirty of the soot in the rain! Kids nowadays don't know they are born! ;o)

replies.author: @ecospider5
replies.text: Producing and storing hydrogen is extremely hard. That’s why it has not taken over ice vehicles.

replies.author: @rfiskillingussoftly6568

replies.author: @nick_7869
replies.text: @ecospider5 the modern designs for hydrogen refueling stations are quite simple. They only store small amounts for expected demand and top up on the go, refilling their reserve. Refueling is fast, even compared to petrol and diesel. The environmental impact however, like ev's, depends on the fuel used to make the electricity to power them. Like ev's, they can use more power over night to fill the tanks when there's less demand for electricity. Although, as ev' numbers increase, the night time demand will rise giving the national grid a new headache.

author: @philipvecchio3292
text:
My family has a diary from a relative that lived from the early 1800s until the late 1800s in Philadelphia. He Was really interested in science, grinding, his own lenses and things like that, eventually buying the process for making phosphor bronze from inventors in England.

One of the things that had amazed him was when the street lamps switched from whale oil over to coal gas. It was a brighter, more consistent source of light within the city for much less money, and the city built out coal gas infrastructure.

Coal Gas was a massive source of light and power in urban areas in particular.

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: Along with the gas mantle of course invented I believe by a Swede and was the first application of the lanthanides , called rare earths these days.

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: A treatise or PhD has been done on the impact of lighting on civilization and the relative cost of effective lighting through the ages . Led lighting replaced kerosene almost overnight and a drop in oil price was concurrent .
